lessons.title: Venous anatomy & physiology
objectives: Map the superficial veins of the upper limb used for IV access
Differentiate a vein from an artery and a nerve at the bedside
Explain the three tunica layers and venous valves — and why they matter
Justify why forearm veins are the preferred target
lessons.lead: The vein you choose determines the entire insertion. Before you ever pick up a catheter, you need a working map of the arm's venous network and a clear sense of what tissue your needle is passing through.

sections.h: The venous map of the arm
p: Peripheral IVs are placed in the superficial veins of the hand and forearm, which sit just under the skin in the subcutaneous tissue. Knowing each vessel's location and behavior lets you plan a stick rather than hunt for one.
list: Cephalic vein — runs up the radial (thumb) side of the forearm; large, straight, and well-supported. An excellent first-choice target.
Basilic vein — the medial (little-finger) forearm; large but rolls easily and sits near a nerve and the brachial artery higher up.
Median cubital vein — crosses the antecubital fossa (the bend of the elbow) linking cephalic and basilic; easy but overlies a joint and lies near the brachial artery and median nerve.
Median antebrachial vein — mid-forearm; avoid its portion at the ventral wrist, where nerves run superficially.
Dorsal metacarpal veins — the back of the hand; small, mobile, and prone to infiltration. Acceptable but not first choice in adults.

note.t: Work distal-to-proximal and favor the forearm — straight, well-supported veins away from joints give a stable line and preserve proximal veins for future access.

p: Three structures live close together in the arm, and mistaking one for another causes real harm. Learn to tell them apart before you advance a needle.
list: Vein — soft, spongy, compressible, non-pulsatile; refills after the tourniquet; dark, non-pulsatile blood on entry.
Artery — pulsatile, deeper, thicker-walled; bright-red, pulsatile flashback. If you see this, remove immediately and hold firm pressure.
Nerve — not a vessel you can see; contact causes sudden sharp, shooting, electric pain or radiating numbness. Withdraw at once.

note.t: A rigid, hard, cord-like vein is likely thrombosed or sclerosed — do not use it. A pulsatile vessel is an artery — never your target.

p: Veins have three layers. The innermost tunica intima is a delicate endothelial lining; injuring it — with an oversized catheter, an irritating drug, or repeated movement — triggers phlebitis and thrombosis. The tunica media is smooth muscle that constricts (spasms) with cold or irritation, and the outer tunica adventitia is connective tissue.
Valves sit at branch points and keep blood moving toward the heart, but they stop a catheter from advancing. Choose a straight, unbranched segment and you will thread far more smoothly.

note.t: Absorption and flow physics follow Poiseuille's law — flow rises with the fourth power of the vessel/catheter radius and falls with length. A short, appropriately sized catheter in a good vein outperforms an oversized one that injures the intima.

inter.q: Tap the best vein to cannulate.

fb.good: Correct — a straight, well-supported forearm vein is the ideal target.
fb.valve: That segment sits near a branch/valve — the catheter will snag. Avoid it.
fb.hand: Dorsal hand veins are small and roll — usable, but not the first choice in an adult.

tips: If a vein is visible but not palpable, keep looking — a vein you can feel is far more reliable than one you can only see.
The nondominant arm is usually the kinder choice for the patient and less prone to dislodgement.
Warmth, gravity (lower the arm), and a gentle fist bring veins up without the trauma of slapping.
mistakes: Targeting a pulsatile vessel — that is an artery, not a vein.
Choosing a hard, cord-like (sclerosed) vein that will not thread or flush.
Ignoring valves and branch points, then forcing the catheter when it snags.
Defaulting to the antecubital fossa when a good straight forearm vein is available.

quiz.q: You palpate your intended target and feel a distinct pulse under your finger. What does this tell you?
opts: It's an artery — choose a different site
quiz.why: Pulsatility means artery. Cannulating it risks arterial injury; select a non-pulsatile vein elsewhere.

quiz.q: Why does INS guidance favor a straight forearm vein over the median cubital vein at the elbow when both are available?
opts: The forearm site avoids a flexion point, giving a stable line and preserving the AC fossa
The elbow has no veins worth using
quiz.why: A vein over a joint kinks and occludes with movement; a straight forearm vein is more stable and keeps the AC fossa in reserve.

quiz.q: A catheter threads halfway then stops with mild resistance in an otherwise good vein. The most likely anatomical cause is:
quiz.why: Valves cluster at branch points and block advancement — choosing a straight, unbranched segment prevents this.

takeaways: Forearm cephalic/median veins are the workhorse targets; go distal-to-proximal.
Pulsatile = artery; sharp electric pain = nerve — both mean stop.
Protect the intima: right-sized catheter, straight segment, secure line.
Valves live at branch points — thread through straight, unbranched veins.
lessons.refs: INS Infusion Therapy Standards of Practice (2024, 9th ed.); StatPearls, Peripheral Line Placement.

lessons.title: Equipment & catheter selection
objectives: Select catheter gauge by clinical need using the standard color code
Explain over-the-needle catheter design and the flashback chamber
Assemble the full supply set for a safe, aseptic insertion
Describe safety-engineered sharps and why they are required
lessons.lead: Choosing the right catheter and having every supply ready before you start keeps your insertion window short and your technique clean. The guiding rule: the smallest gauge and shortest catheter that will deliver the therapy.

p: IV catheters are color-coded by gauge (an ISO standard). A larger number means a thinner catheter. Bigger bores deliver faster but injure the vein more, so size to the therapy — not to 'just in case.'

head: Gauge
Color
Typical use
rows: Major trauma, massive/rapid resuscitation, rapid transfusion
Blood products, surgery, contrast, larger-volume fluids
Adult default — most fluids, medications, and blood run well
Older adults, small or fragile veins, routine meds, pediatrics
Neonates, very fragile or thin veins, slow infusions

note.t: A catheter should occupy roughly ≤45% of the vein's diameter so blood still flows around it — this lowers phlebitis and thrombosis risk. Oversizing 'to be safe' backfires.

p: A peripheral IV is an over-the-needle catheter: a soft polyurethane catheter preloaded over a sharp, hollow introducer needle (the stylet). When the needle tip enters the vein, blood appears in the transparent flashback chamber. You then advance the catheter off the stylet into the vein and withdraw the needle.
Gather and open everything before you apply the tourniquet so your aseptic field stays intact and the stick is quick.
list: Single-use, latex-free tourniquet
Skin antiseptic — chlorhexidine gluconate ≥0.5% in alcohol (preferred)
Clean nonsterile gloves
Extension set / J-loop and a needle-free connector (stabilizes the hub, reduces catheter manipulation)
Prefilled 0.9% sodium chloride flush syringes (10 mL barrel to limit pressure)
Sterile transparent semipermeable dressing and securement
Gauze, tape, label, and a sharps container within reach

p: Under OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogens Standard, IV catheters must have an engineered sharps-injury protection mechanism — a shield or retraction that covers the needle on withdrawal. Activate it immediately and never recap.

note.t: Prime the extension set with saline before insertion. A primed, closed system confirms patency after the stick and prevents air entering the line.

tips: Pick the vein first, then the gauge — a 20 G in a good vein beats an 18 G forced into a marginal one.
A short extension set (J-loop) means you manipulate the loop, not the catheter hub, when connecting — fewer dislodged lines.
Keep the sharps container at arm's reach before you start; you should never carry an exposed stylet across the room.
mistakes: Reaching for the biggest gauge by default — it injures the vein and raises phlebitis risk.
Opening supplies after the tourniquet is on, letting it dwell too long while you fumble.
Using a large, forceful syringe that generates high pressure on flush.
Failing to prime the extension set, so air sits in the line.

quiz.q: A stable adult needs maintenance fluids and routine IV antibiotics, with average forearm veins. Best default gauge?
opts: 20 G — adequate for fluids, meds, and blood if needed
quiz.why: 20 G handles fluids, medications, and blood while sparing the vein; 16 G is oversized for this need and 24 G is unnecessarily slow.

quiz.q: Why does the catheter-to-vein ratio matter?
opts: It doesn't — fill the vein completely
Keeping the catheter to ≤~45% of vein diameter preserves blood flow around it and lowers phlebitis risk
A wider catheter always prevents infiltration
quiz.why: Blood flowing around the catheter dilutes irritants and reduces endothelial injury; a catheter that fills the vein promotes phlebitis and thrombosis.

quiz.q: Immediately after withdrawing the stylet, you should:
opts: Set it on the bed while you dress the site
Activate the safety shield and drop it in the sharps container now
quiz.why: OSHA requires engaging the safety mechanism and disposing immediately; recapping and setting down are needlestick hazards.

takeaways: Smallest gauge, shortest catheter that does the job.
20 G is the adult default; step up only for blood/rapid volume, down for fragile veins.
Prime and assemble everything before the tourniquet goes on.
Engage the sharps safety and bin it the instant the stylet is out.
lessons.refs: INS Standards of Practice (2024); OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1030; Lippincott IV Catheter Sizes & Flow Rates.

lessons.title: Patient assessment & consent
objectives: State the indications for peripheral IV access
Identify contraindicated limbs and sites and explain why
Screen for relevant allergies and obtain informed consent
Recognize difficult access and escalate appropriately
lessons.lead: Good assessment prevents most IV problems. Before you choose a vein, confirm the line is indicated, the limb is safe to use, and the patient understands and consents.

p: Peripheral IV access is used for IV fluids and electrolytes, IV medications, blood products, contrast, resuscitation, and when a patient cannot take oral intake. If you can draw initial labs at insertion, do so before infusing to avoid diluting the sample.
There is no absolute contraindication to a peripheral IV, but several limbs and sites carry real risk. Choose another location.
list: AV fistula or graft limb — never use; protect dialysis access.
Same side as a mastectomy or axillary lymph-node dissection — traditional caution for lymphedema. Newer evidence questions an absolute ban, so use clinical judgment and involve the patient.
Affected limb after stroke or with paresis — impaired sensation and circulation; the patient may not feel a complication.
Cellulitis, burns, open wounds, or a planned surgical field at or near the site.
Lower extremities in adults — higher risk of thrombophlebitis and embolism; move any leg line to an arm as soon as possible.

p: Screen specifically for chlorhexidine (use an iodophor or 70% alcohol alternative), adhesive/tape, and latex (use latex-free supplies). Explain the procedure, address needle anxiety, obtain consent, and position the patient reclined to reduce vasovagal risk.

Difficult IV access (DIVA) is predictable: a history of IV drug use, chemotherapy-damaged veins, obesity, edema, dehydration, shock, chronic illness, or darker skin tone that obscures veins. Don't turn one patient into a pincushion.

note.t: Two attempts per clinician, then escalate to a more skilled colleague or the vascular access team with ultrasound. Repeated blind sticks damage veins and erode trust.

tips: Ask the patient — many know exactly which vein 'always works' or which arm to avoid.
For known DIVA, go to ultrasound or the vascular access team early rather than after several failures.
Position anxious or needle-phobic patients reclined from the start; it is far easier than managing a faint mid-stick.
mistakes: Using the arm on the side of a mastectomy or AV fistula without checking.
Skipping the chlorhexidine allergy question, then prepping with it.
Exceeding two attempts before escalating.
Infusing fluids before drawing labs, diluting the sample.

quiz.q: A patient with end-stage renal disease has a left-arm AV fistula and poor right-arm veins. Where do you place the IV?
opts: The fistula arm — the veins are bigger
The right arm, using ultrasound/vascular access support if needed
quiz.why: The fistula limb is off-limits to protect dialysis access; escalate for support on the other arm rather than risk the fistula.

quiz.q: You've attempted twice on a patient with a history of IV drug use and failed both times. Best next step?
opts: Try a third and fourth time
Escalate to ultrasound-guided placement or the vascular access team
Use the largest gauge to guarantee success
quiz.why: After two failed attempts, standards call for escalation; DIVA warrants visualization technology, not more blind attempts.

quiz.q: Blood work is needed and an IV is being started. To keep the sample accurate you should:
opts: Start fluids, then draw from the line later
Draw the initial labs at insertion before infusing anything
quiz.why: Infusing first dilutes the specimen; drawing at insertion before any infusion preserves accuracy.

takeaways: Confirm the line is indicated and the limb is safe before choosing a vein.
Fistula and mastectomy-side limbs are off-limits (mastectomy: judgment + patient input).
Screen chlorhexidine, latex, and adhesive allergies; get consent.
Two attempts, then escalate — especially for known difficult access.
lessons.refs: INS Standards of Practice (2024); CDC Intravascular Catheter guidelines; ASBrS ipsilateral-access statement.

lessons.title: Site selection strategy
objectives: Apply the distal-to-proximal, forearm-preferred selection order
List concrete criteria for a good target vein
Name the sites to avoid and the specific reason for each
Use safe vein-dilation techniques
lessons.lead: Site selection is where anatomy meets strategy. Start distal, prefer the forearm, and match the vein to the catheter so the line stays patent and the patient keeps veins in reserve.

p: Begin at the most distal suitable vein and move proximal only as needed, with forearm veins preferred for stability and vein preservation. Reserve the dorsal hand for when the forearm fails, and the antecubital fossa for emergencies or larger-bore needs.

list: Straight, unbranched segment at least as long as the catheter
Soft, springy, and palpable — bounces back when pressed
Away from valves (branch points) and joints
Well-supported by surrounding tissue so it won't roll
Sized so the catheter fills no more than about half the lumen

p: Each of these sites carries a specific, avoidable complication.

head: Avoid
Because
rows: Kinking, positional occlusion, mechanical phlebitis, dislodgement
Superficial nerves — high risk of nerve injury and severe pain
Affected limbs (fistula, mastectomy side, paresis, DVT)
Sclerosed, bruised, or previously infiltrated veins
Won't thread or flush; higher failure and complication rates

p: Apply the tourniquet 4–6 inches (10–15 cm) above the site, snug enough to impede venous but not arterial flow — the radial pulse should stay palpable. Let the arm hang dependent, apply warmth for a minute, and have the patient make a gentle fist.

note.t: No hard slapping and no vigorous fist-pumping — slapping bruises, and pumping can falsely elevate potassium on labs drawn from the site.

inter.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 340 190"><rect x="18" y="55" width="304" height="80" rx="40" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><path d="M40 100 C130 78,210 116,300 92" stroke="#5f8ea0" stroke-width="7" fill="none" opacity=".55"/><path d="M40 118 C140 134,215 104,300 124" stroke="#5f8ea0" stroke-width="5" fill="none" opacity=".4"/><circle class="hot" data-id="good" cx="158" cy="106" r="13" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/><circle class="hot" data-id="valve" cx="245" cy="96" r="13" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/><circle class="hot" data-id="hand" cx="62" cy="103" r="13" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/></svg>
inter.q: You need a stable line for multi-day antibiotics. Tap the best target.

fb.good: Correct — a straight forearm vein gives the most stable, longest-lasting line.
fb.valve: Near a branch/valve — the catheter will snag and the line may fail early.
fb.hand: A hand vein works short-term but is fragile for multi-day therapy.

tips: Think ahead: if therapy will run several days, a forearm vein outlasts a hand vein.
If the only palpable vein crosses a joint, dilate other veins first or escalate — a flexion-site line often fails within hours.
A tourniquet left on too long hemoconcentrates the limb and actually makes veins harder to read.
mistakes: Starting proximal and 'using up' good veins, leaving nothing distal.
Cannulating the ventral wrist and striking a nerve.
Placing an adult leg IV and leaving it there.
Leaving the tourniquet on for minutes while you deliberate.

quiz.q: Therapy is expected to last five days. Which choice best preserves the line and the patient's veins?
opts: Dorsal hand vein — easy to see
quiz.why: A straight forearm vein is stable for multi-day therapy and avoids the flexion problems of the AC fossa and the fragility of hand veins.

quiz.q: The only visible vein crosses the inner (ventral) wrist. The safest response is:
opts: Use it — a visible vein is a good vein
Avoid it (nerve-injury risk) and dilate other veins or escalate
Use a larger gauge to stabilize it
quiz.why: The ventral wrist overlies superficial nerves; it risks nerve injury and severe pain — choose another vein or get help.

quiz.q: A tourniquet that blanches the limb and obliterates the radial pulse is:
opts: Too tight — it impedes arterial inflow and hemoconcentrates the limb
Fine as long as it's quick
quiz.why: The tourniquet should impede venous return only; occluding the artery prevents filling and distorts labs.

takeaways: Distal-to-proximal, forearm-preferred, straight and unbranched.
Match catheter to vein size (~≤45% of lumen).
Avoid flexion points, the ventral wrist, and adult leg veins.
Tourniquet snug with a palpable pulse; dilate with warmth and gravity, not slapping.
lessons.refs: INS Standards of Practice (2024); StatPearls, Peripheral Line Placement.

lessons.title: Preparation & asepsis
objectives: Apply hand hygiene at the correct moments
Use aseptic non-touch technique (ANTT)
Perform skin antisepsis with correct agent, friction, and dry time
Explain why the prepped site must not be re-palpated
lessons.lead: Catheter-related infection is largely preventable at the moment of insertion. Clean hands, a protected key site, and proper skin antisepsis are the difference between a safe line and a bloodstream infection.

p: Perform hand hygiene — soap and water or alcohol-based rub — before and after palpating the site and before and after insertion. Use aseptic non-touch technique: identify the 'key parts' (catheter tip, connector, syringe tip) and the 'key site' (the prepped skin) and never touch them. Clean nonsterile gloves are appropriate for a short peripheral IV as long as you do not re-touch the prepped skin.

The preferred agent is chlorhexidine gluconate ≥0.5% in alcohol. If the skin is visibly soiled, wash with soap and water first. Apply with friction — a back-and-forth scrub for about 30 seconds — then let it air-dry completely. Do not fan or blot it.

note.t: The antimicrobial kill happens as the antiseptic dries. Chlorhexidine-alcohol dries in about 30 seconds; povidone-iodine needs 1.5–2 minutes. Puncturing wet antiseptic both stings and skips the disinfection step.

p: Once the skin is prepped and dry, do not re-palpate the vein unless you re-prep or maintain sterile technique. Re-touching recontaminates the key site and defeats the antisepsis you just performed.

note.t: If you must re-locate the vein after prepping, either re-prep the skin or palpate only above/below the prepped zone — never on the puncture point.

tips: Prep, then set up your last supplies while the antiseptic dries — you use the dry time productively instead of rushing the stick.
If a patient reports stinging on puncture, the antiseptic was likely still wet — a reminder to wait for full dry.
Alcohol-based hand rub is faster and generally more effective than soap and water when hands aren't visibly soiled.
mistakes: Puncturing before the antiseptic has dried.
Fanning or wiping the site dry, which recontaminates it.
Re-palpating the prepped vein with an ungloved or unprepped finger.
Skipping hand hygiene between patients or before touching the site.

quiz.q: You scrub with chlorhexidine-alcohol, then immediately insert because you're in a hurry. What's the risk?
opts: None — the scrub is what matters
Reduced disinfection and stinging, because kill occurs as it dries
quiz.why: Antimicrobial action happens during drying; puncturing wet antiseptic skips disinfection and causes stinging.

quiz.q: After prepping, you lose the vein visually and want to feel for it again. The correct action is:
opts: Palpate directly on the prepped puncture site
Re-prep the site or palpate only outside the prepped zone
quiz.why: Re-touching the key site recontaminates it; re-prep or palpate away from the puncture point to maintain asepsis.

quiz.q: For a routine short peripheral IV, appropriate gloves are:
opts: Clean nonsterile gloves, as long as the prepped site isn't re-touched
quiz.why: ANTT allows clean gloves for short PIVs provided the key site is protected; standard precautions still require gloves.

takeaways: Hand hygiene before/after palpation and insertion.
Chlorhexidine-alcohol, 30-second friction scrub, full air-dry.
Kill happens on drying — never puncture wet antiseptic.
Prepped site is sacred: don't re-touch it.
lessons.refs: CDC Intravascular Catheter & Hand Hygiene guidelines; WHO injection safety; INS Standards (2024).

lessons.title: The insertion, step by step
objectives: Perform peripheral IV insertion in correct sequence
Explain the rationale behind each step
Interpret first and second flashback
Confirm patency and secure the line
lessons.lead: With a vein chosen and the site prepped, the insertion itself is a short, controlled sequence. Understanding the reason for each step is what turns a memorized routine into reliable technique.

p: Each step below has a purpose — the rationale is what lets you adapt when a stick doesn't go to plan.
list: Verify and position — order, two identifiers, consent; arm dependent and supported. Fills the veins, prevents fainting.
Tourniquet 4–6 in above, snug with a pulse present. Engorges the vein without occluding the artery.
Anchor the vein with distal traction from your nondominant thumb. Stops it rolling and pulls the skin taut.
Insert bevel-up at 10–30° and advance slowly. A shallow angle avoids piercing the back wall.
First flashback appears in the chamber. The needle tip is in the vein — but the catheter isn't yet.

Lower the angle, advance 1–2 mm so the catheter (not just the needle) is intraluminal, then thread the catheter off the stylet, watching for a second flashback. Confirms the catheter itself is in the lumen.
Release the tourniquet, apply pressure above the tip, withdraw and activate the stylet safety, and connect the primed extension set.

p: Aspirate for blood return if indicated, then flush gently with 0.9% saline. You want free flow with no swelling, resistance, pain, or leaking. A push-pause ('pulsatile') flush helps clear the lumen.

note.t: Burning, swelling, or resistance on flush means infiltration — stop immediately, do not force, and reassess. Forcing a flush drives fluid into the tissue.

p: Apply a sterile transparent dressing so the site stays visible, add engineered securement, and label with date, time, gauge, and your initials. A visible, secured site is one you can monitor.

tips: A small first flashback with a catheter that won't thread usually means only the needle bevel is in — lower the angle and advance 1–2 mm before threading.
No flashback at all? Withdraw slightly and redirect gently; don't 'fish' side to side, which lacerates the vein.
Never re-advance the stylet into a catheter you've already threaded — it can shear the catheter and embolize.
mistakes: Advancing the catheter after only the first (needle) flashback.
Re-inserting the stylet into a threaded catheter (shear/embolism risk).
Opening the system before releasing the tourniquet, causing a blood spill.
Forcing a flush against resistance and driving an infiltration.

quiz.q: You get a flashback but the catheter won't advance. The most likely reason and fix?
opts: The vein is blown — remove and restart
Only the needle tip is intraluminal — lower the angle and advance 1–2 mm before threading
Push harder at the same angle
quiz.why: The catheter sits slightly behind the needle tip; seating it 1–2 mm further into the lumen before threading solves this without back-walling the vein.

quiz.q: After threading the catheter you notice it isn't fully advanced and reach for the stylet to push it in. This is:
opts: Dangerous — re-advancing the stylet can shear the catheter and embolize it
quiz.why: Re-inserting the needle into a threaded catheter risks slicing off the catheter tip; never do it.

quiz.q: On the confirming flush the site swells and the patient reports burning. You should:
opts: Stop, do not force, and reassess for infiltration
quiz.why: Swelling plus burning signals infiltration; continuing forces fluid into tissue. Stop and reassess.

takeaways: Anchor, bevel-up, shallow angle, watch for two flashbacks.
First flash = needle in; drop the angle and advance before threading.
Release the tourniquet before opening the system.
Confirm free-flowing, painless flush — resistance means stop.
lessons.refs: INS Standards of Practice (2024); StatPearls; Perry, Potter & Ostendorf, Clinical Nursing Skills & Techniques.

lessons.title: Complications & troubleshooting
objectives: Distinguish infiltration from extravasation and manage each
Grade phlebitis and infiltration with the INS scales
Recognize hematoma, nerve injury, and arterial puncture
Take correct first actions for each complication
lessons.lead: Even excellent technique meets complications. What separates a safe clinician is early recognition and the right first move — often 'stop the infusion' before anything else.

p: Infiltration is leakage of a non-vesicant fluid into the tissue: swelling, coolness, blanching, taut skin, tenderness, and a sluggish or stopped flow. Extravasation is leakage of a vesicant (vasopressors, many chemotherapies, calcium, concentrated electrolytes, contrast) and can cause blistering and tissue necrosis — a true emergency.
list: Both: stop the infusion immediately, do not flush, disconnect and try to aspirate residual drug.
Infiltration: remove the catheter, elevate the limb, apply a cool compress (for most non-vesicants), grade and document.
Extravasation: often leave the catheter to aspirate/give antidote per protocol; notify the provider and pharmacy urgently.

note.t: Compress choice differs: cold for most infiltrations, but WARM for vasopressor and vinca-alkaloid extravasations (vasodilation disperses the drug). Phentolamine is the antidote for vasopressor extravasation; hyaluronidase for many hyperosmolar agents.

p: Objective grading guides action and documentation.

head: Phlebitis scale
Finding
rows: Above + a palpable venous cord / streak
Palpable cord >1 in and/or purulent drainage

note.t: Remove the catheter for any phlebitis Grade 2 or higher — a red, tender, corded vein comes out even if it still flushes.

list: Hematoma — from through-and-through puncture or removal without pressure; hold pressure and elevate.
Nerve injury — sharp, electric, radiating pain on insertion; withdraw immediately and document.
Arterial puncture — pulsatile bright-red flashback; remove and hold firm pressure 5+ minutes.
Air embolism — keep the system primed and closed; if suspected, left-lateral Trendelenburg and high-flow oxygen.
Vasovagal (fainting) — insert reclined; if it happens, lower the head and monitor.

tips: Your reflex for any suspected infiltration or extravasation is the same first word: STOP.
Learn which of your common drugs are vesicants before you hang them — it changes your compress and whether you pull the catheter.
A line that flushes fine but shows a red, tender cord is phlebitis — patency does not override the need to remove it.
mistakes: Flushing 'to check' when infiltration is suspected — it worsens tissue injury.
Applying cold to a vasopressor extravasation instead of warmth.
Pulling the catheter on a vesicant extravasation before aspirating/antidote per protocol.
Leaving a Grade 2+ phlebitic line in because it still flushes.

quiz.q: A norepinephrine infusion site becomes blanched, swollen, and painful. Your first actions?
opts: Cold compress and keep the line for antidote
Stop the infusion, keep the catheter, notify pharmacy, prepare phentolamine and a WARM compress
Remove the catheter and flush the tissue
quiz.why: This is vasopressor extravasation: stop, retain the catheter to aspirate/give phentolamine, and use warmth to disperse the drug — cold would worsen ischemia.

quiz.q: An IV flushes freely but the vein is red, tender, and has a palpable cord (Grade 3 phlebitis). You should:
quiz.why: Grade 2+ phlebitis warrants removal regardless of patency; continuing risks worsening inflammation and infection.

quiz.q: The key difference driving management of infiltration vs. extravasation is:
opts: Whether the leaked fluid is a vesicant
quiz.why: Vesicant status determines the emergency response, compress choice, antidote, and whether to keep the catheter.

takeaways: Stop first for any suspected infiltration/extravasation — never flush to check.
Vesicant = extravasation emergency: keep the line, antidote, warm compress for vasopressors.
Grade with the INS scales; remove for phlebitis Grade 2+.
Electric pain = nerve, pulsatile flash = artery — remove and manage.
lessons.refs: INS Phlebitis & Infiltration Scales; Journal of Infusion Nursing; ONS/ASCO extravasation guidance.

lessons.title: Documentation & follow-up
objectives: Document all required elements of an insertion
Monitor the site on the correct schedule
Explain current dwell-time / replacement guidance
Educate the patient on what to report
lessons.lead: The line isn't finished when it's secured. Clear documentation and ongoing monitoring protect the patient and the team — and reflect the standard of care.

list: Date and time
Catheter gauge and length
Exact site (vessel and side)
Number of attempts and who performed them
Use of ultrasound/visualization and any local anesthetic
Patient tolerance and the flush/patency result
Dressing type applied

p: Assess the site at least every shift and with each access — inspect through the transparent dressing and palpate for tenderness or warmth, checking for infiltration and phlebitis with the INS scales. Change the dressing if it is damp, loose, or soiled, and confirm the line is still needed. Remove it promptly when it isn't.

Guidance has shifted. The older rule replaced adult peripheral IVs every 72–96 hours on a schedule. Current INS practice and a large Cochrane review support replacing when clinically indicated — for signs of complication, malfunction, or when no longer needed — rather than on a fixed clock, because routine time-based swaps don't reduce infection or phlebitis. Pediatric lines are replaced only when clinically indicated.

note.t: Always follow your institution's policy, but understand the direction of the evidence: assessment-driven replacement, with prompt removal of any idle or symptomatic line.

tips: Documenting the number of attempts isn't about blame — it flags a difficult-access patient so the next clinician escalates sooner.
A clean, well-flushing line that's no longer needed should still come out; every dwell-day adds risk.
Teach patients the three words that matter: pain, swelling, redness — report any of them.
mistakes: Omitting attempt count or exact site from the record.
Covering the site with an opaque wrap that prevents inspection.
Leaving an idle line in 'just in case.'
Quoting a rigid 72-hour rule as absolute when policy allows clinically-indicated replacement.

quiz.q: A 72-hour-old 20 G forearm line is clean, non-tender, and flushes freely; policy permits clinically-indicated replacement. You should:
opts: Remove and re-site it because it's 72 hours old
Keep it — there are no signs of complication
Remove it only if the patient asks
quiz.why: Current evidence supports clinically-indicated replacement in adults; a healthy line needn't be re-sited on the clock alone.

quiz.q: Which set best represents required insertion documentation?
opts: Time, gauge/length, site, attempts, tolerance, and patency
quiz.why: These elements make the line auditable and safe to hand off; the diagnosis alone is not insertion documentation.

quiz.q: The single most useful thing to teach a patient about their new IV is to report:
opts: Pain, swelling, or redness at the site
quiz.why: Patient-reported pain, swelling, or redness catches infiltration and phlebitis early.

takeaways: Document time, gauge/length, site, attempts, tolerance, patency.
Assess every shift and with each access; keep the site visible.
Evidence favors clinically-indicated, not routine, replacement in adults.
Remove idle lines; teach the patient to report pain/swelling/redness.
lessons.refs: CDC Intravascular Catheter guidelines; INS Standards (2024); Cochrane review on clinically-indicated replacement (2019).

lessons.title: Special populations
objectives: Adapt technique for pediatric and geriatric patients
Approach obese and difficult-access patients
Manage insertion in dehydration and shock
Match device and escalation to the patient
lessons.lead: The fundamentals hold, but veins — and the people attached to them — vary. Small adjustments in gauge, angle, tourniquet, and escalation make the difference.

p: Use smaller gauges (22–24 G; 24–26 G in neonates). Hand and foot veins are common; scalp veins may be used in infants. Prioritize atraumatic care — topical anesthetic, distraction, caregiver presence, swaddling — and near-infrared or ultrasound for difficult access. Replace only when clinically indicated.

Older veins are fragile, mobile ('rolling'), thin-walled, and sit under lax skin, so hematoma risk is high. Use minimal or no tourniquet (or apply it over a sleeve), a low insertion angle (10–15°), a smaller gauge (22–24 G), firm skin traction, and a gentle flush. Watch for fluid overload with reduced cardiac and renal reserve.
Landmarks are obscured; palpate rather than rely on sight, favor the forearm over deep hand veins, and go to ultrasound early. Longer catheters may be needed to reach deeper veins.

Flat, poorly filling veins respond to warmth, dependent positioning, and hydration where feasible; use ultrasound guidance. If access is emergent and peripheral attempts fail, intraosseous access may be indicated.

note.t: Across every population, the two-attempt limit and early escalation still apply — vulnerability is a reason to escalate sooner, not to persist.

tips: In older adults, sometimes the best 'tourniquet' is none at all — gravity plus warmth fills fragile veins without blowing them.
For a frightened child, the setup (warm pack, distraction, a parent's hand) does more for success than any needle trick.
In obesity, don't chase a deep vein blindly — ultrasound turns a repeated-failure patient into a one-stick success.
mistakes: Using a standard tight tourniquet on fragile geriatric veins and blowing them.
A steep insertion angle in a thin-skinned older adult.
Blind, repeated deep sticks in an obese patient instead of using ultrasound.
Forgetting fluid-overload risk when infusing an older adult quickly.

quiz.q: An 88-year-old has fragile, rolling veins under thin skin. Which adjustment set is best?
opts: Tight tourniquet, steep angle, large gauge
Minimal/no tourniquet, low 10–15° angle, smaller gauge, firm traction
No changes — treat like any adult
quiz.why: Fragile veins need less tourniquet pressure, a shallow angle, a smaller catheter, and good anchoring to avoid blowing the vein.

quiz.q: A patient with obesity has had three failed blind attempts by two clinicians. Best next step?
opts: Use ultrasound guidance / vascular access team
quiz.why: Difficult access warrants visualization technology rather than more blind attempts, honoring the attempt limit.

quiz.q: In a hypovolemic patient with flat veins, a helpful first measure is:
opts: Warmth, dependent positioning, and ultrasound guidance
quiz.why: Dilation measures and visualization improve success; if emergent and peripheral access fails, consider intraosseous.

takeaways: Downsize gauge and soften technique for fragile geriatric veins.
Atraumatic care drives pediatric success.
Reach for ultrasound early in obesity and difficult access.
Vulnerability means escalate sooner — the two-attempt limit still holds.
lessons.refs: INS Standards (2024); CDC Intravascular Catheter guidelines; StatPearls.

lessons.title: Practice scenarios & case studies
objectives: Apply IV principles to realistic clinical situations
Prioritize the correct first action under pressure
Integrate assessment, technique, and complication management
lessons.lead: These cases pull the course together. Read each, decide your move, then check the reasoning. The right first action is often the whole answer.

p: You place a 20 G forearm line. On the confirming flush the patient reports burning, you feel resistance, and slight coolness and swelling appear.

note.t: Move: this is infiltration. Stop flushing immediately, do not force, disconnect and gently aspirate, remove the catheter, elevate the limb, apply a cool compress (non-vesicant), grade with the INS scale, document, and restart in a different limb.

sections.h: Case B — Vesicant and a swelling site
p: A patient on IV norepinephrine develops blanching, swelling, and pain at the hand site.

note.t: Move: treat as an extravasation emergency. Stop the infusion, do NOT remove the catheter yet, aspirate residual drug, notify provider and pharmacy, prepare phentolamine and a WARM compress, photograph and measure, and grade. Warmth and phentolamine — not cold — are correct for a vasopressor.

sections.h: Case C — Only vein is over a joint
p: A dehydrated patient's only palpable vein crosses the ventral wrist / antecubital flexion point.

note.t: Move: avoid the ventral wrist (nerve injury) and flexion points. Try dilation (warmth, dependency, hydration) and use ultrasound / the vascular access team to find a straight forearm vein. If a flexion site is truly unavoidable and emergent, use it temporarily with a joint splint and re-site as soon as possible.

quiz.q: Case A recap — burning, resistance, and swelling on flush. The single most important first action is:
opts: Flush harder to clear the line
quiz.why: Stopping first prevents driving more fluid into the tissue; everything else follows from that.

quiz.q: Case B recap — why keep the catheter in a norepinephrine extravasation?
opts: To aspirate residual drug and deliver the antidote (phentolamine) through it
quiz.why: Retaining the catheter allows aspiration and local antidote administration per extravasation protocol.

quiz.q: Case C recap — the reason to avoid the ventral wrist is:
opts: Superficial nerves make nerve injury and severe pain likely
quiz.why: The ventral wrist overlies superficial nerves; cannulating there risks lasting nerve injury.

takeaways: Infiltration and extravasation both start with STOP.
Vesicant extravasation: keep the line, antidote, warm compress for vasopressors.
Protect nerves — avoid the ventral wrist and flexion points.
When the only option is unsafe, dilate and escalate rather than force it.
lessons.refs: INS Standards (2024); Journal of Infusion Nursing; ONS/ASCO extravasation guidance.

lessons.title: Muscle anatomy & physiology
objectives: Explain why medications are given by the IM route
Identify the muscle layers a needle must cross
Name the four IM muscles and their neurovascular hazards
Explain why the dorsogluteal site is now avoided
lessons.lead: Intramuscular injection places medication into well-perfused skeletal muscle for reliable, steady absorption. Getting there safely means knowing the tissue you pass through and the nerves and vessels you must miss.

p: Skeletal muscle is highly vascular, so an IM medication is absorbed faster than subcutaneous but more steadily than IV. IM bypasses first-pass liver metabolism, tolerates larger volumes than the subcutaneous route, and supports depot (sustained-release) drugs such as long-acting antipsychotics, hormones, and benzathine penicillin.

The needle must pass skin, then subcutaneous fat, then deep fascia, and land in the muscle belly. Stop short in fat and absorption becomes slow and erratic — and irritant depot drugs can form sterile abscesses.

list: Deltoid — the routine adult vaccine site; small, so small volumes only. The radial and axillary nerves and the subacromial bursa sit nearby — inject too high and you risk shoulder injury.
Ventrogluteal (gluteus medius/minimus) — the preferred site for most adult IM medications: thick muscle, thin overlying fat, and no major nerves or vessels in the target zone.
Vastus lateralis (outer mid-thigh) — large, safe, and the preferred site for infants; a good adult alternative.
Dorsogluteal (upper-outer buttock) — historically common, now discouraged.

sections.h: Why dorsogluteal fell out of favor
p: The dorsogluteal site sits near the sciatic nerve and the superior gluteal artery, and it has the thickest subcutaneous fat of any site — so injections frequently land in fat rather than muscle. Studies show only a minority of intended dorsogluteal injections are truly intramuscular, and the site produces more pain, bleeding, and nerve-injury risk than the ventrogluteal.

note.t: The ventrogluteal preference is widely taught best-practice consensus; the safety-superiority evidence is strong but not large-RCT level. Teach ventrogluteal first, and know why.

tips: Match the drug to the tissue: irritant, oily, or depot medications belong in the thick ventrogluteal muscle, not the small deltoid.
If you can't confidently name the nerve you're avoiding at a site, that's a signal to slow down and re-landmark.
Absorption depends on actually reaching muscle — the most common failure is depositing in fat with too short a needle.
mistakes: Defaulting to the dorsogluteal site out of habit.
Treating the deltoid as a general-purpose site for any volume.
Assuming any gluteal injection reaches muscle regardless of body fat.
Choosing a site without accounting for the drug's irritancy or volume.

quiz.q: An irritant 3 mL depot antipsychotic is ordered. Which muscle best fits the drug's volume and irritancy?
quiz.why: The ventrogluteal is thick, accommodates up to ~3 mL, and has no major neurovasculature — ideal for irritant depot medications.

quiz.q: The main safety reason to avoid the dorsogluteal site is:
opts: Proximity to the sciatic nerve/gluteal vessels and frequent fat-only deposition
quiz.why: Sciatic-nerve risk plus low true-IM success (thick fat) make ventrogluteal the safer choice.

quiz.q: Compared with subcutaneous injection, IM absorption is:
opts: Faster and steadier because muscle is well perfused
quiz.why: Muscle's rich blood supply gives faster, more consistent uptake than the poorly vascular subcutaneous layer.

takeaways: IM = fast, steady absorption in well-perfused muscle.
Four sites: deltoid, ventrogluteal (preferred adult), vastus lateralis, dorsogluteal (avoid).
Dorsogluteal risks the sciatic nerve and often lands in fat.
Match irritant/large-volume drugs to the ventrogluteal.
lessons.refs: StatPearls, Intramuscular Injection; CDC Pink Book; ventrogluteal vs dorsogluteal meta-analysis.

lessons.title: Needle, syringe & drawing up
objectives: Select needle gauge and length by site and body size
Use the CDC needle-length guidance for adults
Draw up from ampules and vials safely
Explain why the needle is changed after drawing up
lessons.lead: Reaching muscle — not fat — depends on choosing the right needle length for the site and the patient's body size. The wrong length is the most common reason an 'IM' injection lands subcutaneously.

p: IM injections typically use a 22–25 G needle — lower gauge (larger bore) for viscous or oily depot medications, higher gauge for thin aqueous drugs. Match syringe size to the volume, usually 1–3 mL.
Length is chosen so the needle reaches muscle through the overlying fat. For deltoid injections, CDC guidance scales with weight:

head: Adult
Deltoid needle length
rows: 5/8 in (16 mm) — only at 90° with skin not bunched

note.t: The 5/8-inch needle only qualifies as IM at 90° with the skin NOT bunched. Ventrogluteal/gluteal injections in adults commonly need a 1½-inch needle, especially at higher BMI.

p: Use a filter or blunt fill needle to draw from a glass ampule (prevents aspirating glass fragments) and to reconstitute from vials, then change to a fresh needle before injecting. A clean, sharp needle free of medication on its outside causes less pain and tissue tracking.

note.t: Snap ampules with the neck wrapped in gauze, breaking away from you. Do not routinely add an air bubble to a standard IM syringe.

tips: When in doubt on an average-or-larger adult gluteal injection, a 1½-inch needle is the safer bet against a fat-only deposit.
Filter needles are cheap insurance against glass microparticles from ampules — swap to a clean needle to inject.
A needle dulled by piercing a rubber stopper hurts more going in; changing it improves comfort.
mistakes: Using a 5/8-inch needle at an angle or with bunched skin (lands subcutaneous).
Injecting with the same needle used to pierce a vial stopper.
Drawing from an ampule without a filter needle.
Choosing needle length by habit rather than site and body size.

quiz.q: A 240-lb (>118 kg) man needs a 0.5 mL flu vaccine in the deltoid. Best needle length?
quiz.why: At >118 kg a 1½-inch needle is needed to reach deltoid muscle through the overlying fat.

quiz.q: You draw a medication from a glass ampule. The correct technique is to:
opts: Use any needle and inject with it
Draw with a filter/blunt needle, then change to a fresh needle to inject
Draw with the injecting needle to save time
quiz.why: A filter needle prevents aspirating glass shards; changing the needle keeps the injecting needle sharp and clean.

quiz.q: A 5/8-inch needle can be counted on to reach muscle only when it is inserted:
opts: At 90° with the skin not bunched
quiz.why: The short needle only qualifies as IM at a true 90° with flat, unbunched skin.

takeaways: 22–25 G; length scales with site and body size.
Deltoid length climbs with weight; gluteal often needs 1½ in.
Filter needle from ampules; change the needle before injecting.
5/8 in is IM only at 90° with unbunched skin.
lessons.refs: CDC Vaccine Administration & Needle Length; Immunize.org; StatPearls.

lessons.title: Patient assessment & the rights
objectives: Confirm the rights of medication administration
Identify contraindications and cautions for IM injection
Screen allergies and obtain consent
Position the patient for a relaxed muscle
lessons.lead: Safe IM administration starts before the needle: verify the medication, confirm the patient can safely receive an IM injection, and position them so the muscle is relaxed.

p: Verify the right patient, drug, dose, route, and time, extended in nursing practice to the right site, documentation, reason, response, and the patient's right to refuse. Check the medication against the order and the patient's allergies every time.

list: Active infection, cellulitis, or dermatitis at the site
Significant muscle atrophy (delayed absorption, higher neurovascular risk)
Thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy, or therapeutic anticoagulation — bleeding and hematoma risk (relative contraindication)
Hypovolemic shock — poor perfusion makes absorption erratic
Known allergy to the drug

note.t: For a patient on anticoagulation, clarify the order and consider an alternate route; if IM is truly required, use the smallest gauge, the ventrogluteal, and prolonged firm pressure without massage.

p: Explain the injection and obtain consent; for vaccines, provide the Vaccine Information Statement first. Position for a relaxed muscle, which reduces pain: ventrogluteal side-lying with the upper knee flexed; vastus lateralis supine or sitting; deltoid with the arm relaxed and the hand resting on the hip or lap — never tensed.

tips: A tensed muscle hurts more and resists the needle — a relaxed arm or a side-lying hip position does half the comfort work for you.
Anticoagulated patient? Question whether IM is the right route before you draw up, not after.
For vaccines, the VIS goes to the patient before the injection, not after.
mistakes: Skipping the allergy check against the specific drug.
Giving an IM injection to an anticoagulated patient without clarifying the order.
Injecting into a tensed, guarded muscle.
Omitting the VIS before a vaccine.

quiz.q: A patient on warfarin with an INR of 3.6 is ordered an IM injection. Your first action is:
opts: Give it in the deltoid quickly
Clarify the order / consider an alternate route; if IM is unavoidable, use ventrogluteal, small gauge, prolonged pressure, no massage
quiz.why: Therapeutic anticoagulation is a relative contraindication; clarify first and minimize bleeding risk if IM must proceed.

quiz.q: Positioning the patient with the muscle relaxed primarily:
quiz.why: A relaxed muscle is less painful to inject and lets the needle enter smoothly.

quiz.q: Which is part of the 'rights' beyond patient, drug, dose, route, and time?
opts: Right site and right to refuse
quiz.why: Right site, documentation, reason, response, and the right to refuse extend the core five rights.

takeaways: Verify all rights and the allergy list before every injection.
Anticoagulation/coagulopathy is a relative contraindication — clarify.
Provide the VIS before vaccines.
Relaxed muscle = less pain and easier entry.
lessons.refs: StatPearls, Intramuscular Injection; CDC Vaccine Administration.

lessons.title: Site selection & landmarking
objectives: Landmark the ventrogluteal, deltoid, and vastus lateralis precisely
Match the site to age and volume
State the maximum volume per site
Explain why deltoid landmarking prevents shoulder injury
lessons.lead: Precise landmarking is the heart of safe IM injection. Each site has a defined anatomical target that keeps the needle in muscle and away from nerves and vessels.

list: Ventrogluteal — place your palm on the greater trochanter, index finger on the anterior superior iliac spine, and spread your middle finger back along the iliac crest. Inject into the center of the V between your fingers at 90°. Use the hand opposite the patient's side.
Deltoid — inject into the densest mid-deltoid, 2–3 fingerbreadths (about 2 in / 5 cm) below the acromion, above the level of the axilla, forming an inverted triangle.
Vastus lateralis — the middle third of the outer thigh, on the line from the greater trochanter to the lateral knee.

p: Choose the site for the patient and the volume, then confirm the muscle can accommodate the dose.

head: Patient / need
Site
Max volume
rows: Up to ~1–2 mL by muscle size

note.t: Split volumes above 3 mL into two injections/sites. Injecting more than ~1–2 mL into the small deltoid causes pain, poor absorption, and leakage.

p: Injecting too high, near the acromion, is the classic cause of SIRVA (shoulder injury related to vaccine administration) — deep shoulder pain and limited motion from bursa/tendon involvement. Land 2–3 fingerbreadths below the acromion, every time.

inter.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 340 210"><circle cx="80" cy="34" r="16" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><rect x="58" y="54" width="44" height="86" rx="16" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><rect x="40" y="60" width="20" height="64" rx="10" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><rect x="64" y="138" width="34" height="60" rx="14" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><ellipse class="hot" data-id="deltoid" cx="50" cy="72" rx="12" ry="14" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/><ellipse class="hot" data-id="ventro" cx="88" cy="150" rx="13" ry="12" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/><ellipse class="hot" data-id="vastus" cx="80" cy="176" rx="12" ry="15" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/><text x="150" y="70" fill="#E3CE9A" font-size="12" font-family="Inter">Deltoid — small volumes</text><text x="150" y="150" fill="#E3CE9A" font-size="12" font-family="Inter">Ventrogluteal — preferred</text><text x="150" y="180" fill="#E3CE9A" font-size="12" font-family="Inter">Vastus lateralis — thigh</text></svg>
inter.q: An adult needs a 3 mL IM antibiotic. Tap the preferred site.

fb.ventro: Correct — the ventrogluteal accommodates up to 3 mL with no major neurovasculature.
fb.deltoid: The deltoid is too small for 3 mL — it's for small volumes and vaccines.
fb.vastus: The vastus works, but the ventrogluteal is preferred for adults.

tips: Build the ventrogluteal 'V' with your hand every time — it's faster and more reliable than eyeballing the hip.
If a volume exceeds what the chosen muscle can hold, split it rather than overfill — comfort and absorption both improve.
For the deltoid, find the acromion first and count down; never inject to the naked-eye 'middle of the arm.'
mistakes: Injecting more than ~1–2 mL into the deltoid.
Landmarking the deltoid too high, risking SIRVA.
Guessing the ventrogluteal site instead of building the V.
Putting a large adult volume in the vastus of a small patient.

quiz.q: A patient develops deep shoulder pain and limited range of motion 24 hours after a deltoid vaccine. The likely cause and its prevention?
opts: SIRVA from a too-high injection — landmark 2–3 fingerbreadths below the acromion
quiz.why: SIRVA results from injecting near the acromion into the bursa/tendon; correct landmarking below the acromion prevents it.

quiz.q: You must give 3 mL of an antibiotic IM to an adult. Best site?
quiz.why: Only the ventrogluteal reliably holds 3 mL with minimal neurovascular risk.

quiz.q: When building the ventrogluteal landmark, your palm rests on the:
quiz.why: Palm on the greater trochanter, index to the ASIS, middle finger along the crest — inject in the V.

takeaways: Ventrogluteal is the preferred adult site (up to 3 mL).
Deltoid: small volumes only, 2–3 fingerbreadths below the acromion.
Vastus lateralis is the infant site and an adult alternative.
Split volumes >3 mL; too-high deltoid injections cause SIRVA.
lessons.refs: StatPearls; CDC Pink Book; SIRVA review; Perry, Potter & Ostendorf.

lessons.title: Preparation & asepsis
objectives: Perform hand hygiene and skin antisepsis correctly
Apply the injection-safety 'one needle, one syringe' rule
Reconstitute medication aseptically
Explain the alcohol-prep dry-time rule
lessons.lead: IM injections are a clean procedure with a few non-negotiables: clean hands, a new sterile needle and syringe every time, and antiseptic that is allowed to dry.

p: Perform hand hygiene before and after, and wear gloves per standard precautions. Swab the site with 70% isopropyl alcohol in a circular motion and let it fully dry (about 30 seconds) before inserting. Wet alcohol stings and hasn't yet disinfected.

note.t: Where skin is visibly clean, some guidance permits omitting antisepsis, but US nursing practice standardizes alcohol prep with full dry time. Follow local policy — and always let it dry.

p: Use a new sterile syringe and needle from an intact package for every injection, and use safety-engineered devices. Never reuse or share equipment between patients.
Follow the manufacturer's diluent and volume, mix gently, and use aseptic non-touch technique. Change to a fresh needle after drawing up and before injecting.

tips: Let the alcohol dry while you re-confirm the drug and dose — the pause improves both asepsis and safety.
A patient who says 'that stings going in' often had a wet-alcohol prep — a cue to wait next time.
One needle, one syringe, one patient — every time, no exceptions.
mistakes: Injecting through wet alcohol.
Reusing a needle or syringe across patients.
Skipping the needle change after reconstitution.
Contaminating the vial top or needle by touch.

quiz.q: You swab with alcohol and inject immediately. The consequence is:
opts: Stinging and incomplete antisepsis — the alcohol hasn't dried
quiz.why: Alcohol disinfects as it dries; injecting while wet stings and skips disinfection.

quiz.q: The injection-safety standard for equipment is:
opts: Reuse the syringe with a new needle for the same clinic day
A new sterile syringe and needle for every injection
quiz.why: One needle, one syringe, only one time — never reuse or share.

quiz.q: After reconstituting a medication from a vial, before injecting you should:
quiz.why: A fresh needle is sharper, cleaner, and less painful than one dulled by the stopper.

takeaways: Hand hygiene, gloves, and alcohol prep with full dry time.
New sterile needle and syringe for every injection.
Reconstitute aseptically; change the needle before injecting.
Never inject through wet alcohol.
lessons.refs: WHO Best Practices for Injections; CDC injection safety; StatPearls.

lessons.title: Z-track technique & aspiration
objectives: Perform the Z-track method and explain its purpose
Insert at the correct angle and speed
Apply current aspiration guidance correctly
Withdraw and aftercare the site safely
lessons.lead: Modern IM technique centers on the Z-track method, a 90-degree insertion, and a slow, controlled injection. Two areas are commonly misunderstood — aspiration and injection speed — so we cover them precisely.

p: Z-track is now best practice for all IM injections. With your nondominant hand, pull the skin and subcutaneous tissue about 2.5–3.5 cm laterally and hold it. Insert, inject, wait, withdraw, then release the skin — which creates a zig-zag track that seals the medication in muscle and prevents it leaking back into the subcutaneous tissue.

list: Insert at 90° with a quick dart motion.
Inject slowly — about 10 seconds per mL — to let muscle fibers accommodate the volume.
Wait about 10 seconds with the needle in place, then withdraw at the same angle.
Release the Z-track skin and apply gentle pressure with dry gauze — do not massage.
Activate the sharps safety and dispose immediately.

p: This is where practice has changed. Do NOT aspirate for vaccines — there are no large vessels at the recommended sites, and aspiration adds pain. For most other IM medications, routine aspiration is not required either; it is still taught for the dorsogluteal site or when a manufacturer specifies it.

note.t: If you do aspirate (e.g., dorsogluteal) and blood returns, withdraw, discard, and restart with fresh equipment — you've entered a vessel.

tips: Slow is comfortable: rushing a viscous depot injection is one of the biggest avoidable causes of injection pain.
Don't aspirate a vaccine — it's not just unnecessary, it makes the shot hurt more.
Release the Z-track skin only after the needle is out — that's what seals the track.
mistakes: Skipping Z-track, letting medication leak into subcutaneous tissue.
Aspirating vaccines (unnecessary and more painful).
Injecting faster than ~10 seconds/mL.
Massaging the site after a depot or vaccine injection.

quiz.q: A nurse aspirates for 10 seconds before giving a COVID-19 vaccine in the deltoid. Is this correct?
opts: No — CDC advises against aspiration for vaccines; it adds pain with no benefit
quiz.why: There are no large vessels at recommended vaccine sites, so aspiration is unnecessary and increases pain.

quiz.q: The purpose of the Z-track method is to:
opts: Seal medication in the muscle so it can't leak back to the surface
quiz.why: Offsetting the skin entry from the muscle entry creates a track that closes when released, sealing the drug in muscle.

quiz.q: The recommended IM injection speed is about:
quiz.why: Roughly 10 seconds per mL lets the muscle accommodate the volume, reducing pain and leakage.

takeaways: Z-track for all IM injections; release the skin after withdrawal.
Insert at 90°, inject ~10 sec/mL, don't massage.
No aspiration for vaccines; reserve it for dorsogluteal/manufacturer-specified.
Blood on aspiration = withdraw, discard, restart.
lessons.refs: CDC Vaccine Administration; StatPearls; OpenRN Nursing Skills (Z-track).

lessons.title: Complications & troubleshooting
objectives: Recognize nerve injury and SIRVA
Manage abscess, hematoma, and inadvertent misplacement
Differentiate vasovagal syncope from anaphylaxis
Take correct first actions for each
lessons.lead: IM complications range from local (abscess, hematoma) to serious (nerve injury, anaphylaxis). Recognition and the right first action — especially telling a faint from anaphylaxis — are essential.

p: Sciatic nerve injury from a dorsogluteal injection can cause immediate pain and foot drop — a key reason to use the ventrogluteal. In the deltoid, SIRVA (from too-high injection into the bursa/tendon) causes deep shoulder pain and limited motion within about 48 hours. Prevent both with correct site and landmarking; manage with assessment, referral, and — for SIRVA — NSAIDs, physical therapy, and orthopedic input.

head: Problem
Prevent / manage
rows: Aseptic technique, correct depth, rotate sites; warm compress, I&D + antibiotics if infected
Screen anticoagulation, small gauge, firm pressure without massage
Correct needle length for BMI, 90°, Z-track
Rotate sites, correct volume, slow injection

p: A vasovagal faint — pallor, sweating, dizziness, brief loss of consciousness — is managed by laying the patient supine with legs elevated and monitoring; recovery is quick. Anaphylaxis — urticaria, wheeze, angioedema, hypotension within minutes — is an emergency.

note.t: For anaphylaxis: give IM epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg (1 mg/mL) into the anterolateral thigh, call for help, support airway and circulation, and repeat every 5–15 minutes as needed. Observe vaccine recipients ~15 minutes so you catch reactions.

tips: The fastest way to prevent the worst IM complication (sciatic injury) is simply to use the ventrogluteal instead of the dorsogluteal.
Distinguish the faint from anaphylaxis by the skin and lungs: hives and wheeze point to anaphylaxis and epinephrine.
Keep epinephrine and an emergency plan within reach whenever you give vaccines or first-dose medications.
mistakes: Attributing early anaphylaxis to 'just a faint' and delaying epinephrine.
Using the dorsogluteal and risking sciatic injury.
Massaging a site and worsening a hematoma in an anticoagulated patient.
Skipping the 15-minute post-vaccine observation.

quiz.q: Minutes after an injection a patient has hives, wheezing, and a falling blood pressure. This is:
opts: Vasovagal syncope — lay them down and wait
Anaphylaxis — give IM epinephrine and call for help
quiz.why: Urticaria, wheeze, and hypotension indicate anaphylaxis, which requires immediate IM epinephrine, not simple positioning.

quiz.q: A young adult turns pale and sweaty and briefly faints right after an injection, with no rash or wheeze. Best action?
opts: Lay supine with legs elevated and monitor
quiz.why: This pattern is vasovagal syncope; positioning and monitoring suffice, reserving epinephrine for true anaphylaxis.

quiz.q: The most effective way to prevent sciatic nerve injury from an IM injection is to:
opts: Use the ventrogluteal site instead of the dorsogluteal
quiz.why: Choosing the ventrogluteal avoids the sciatic nerve entirely, unlike the dorsogluteal.

takeaways: Ventrogluteal prevents the sciatic-injury risk of dorsogluteal.
Correct deltoid landmarking prevents SIRVA.
Faint = supine and monitor; anaphylaxis = IM epinephrine now.
Observe vaccine recipients ~15 minutes.
lessons.refs: StatPearls; SIRVA review; CDC anaphylaxis management guidance.

lessons.title: Documentation & follow-up
objectives: Document all required elements, including vaccine specifics
Apply the post-injection observation period
Educate the patient on expected effects and red flags
Record site rotation for repeated injections
lessons.lead: Documentation closes the loop and, for vaccines, meets legal requirements. It also flags site rotation and the red-flag symptoms patients should watch for.

list: Date/time, medication, dose, route (IM), and exact site (and side)
Needle gauge and length, patient position, and technique (Z-track)
Whether aspiration was performed
Patient response/tolerance and education provided

p: For vaccines, also record the manufacturer, lot number, expiration date, the VIS name/edition and date given, and the administering clinician — these are legally required. Observe the recipient about 15 minutes (30 minutes if there's a history of anaphylaxis) for immediate reactions.

Advise on expected soreness and the red flags to report: spreading redness, fever, or severe/persistent shoulder pain (possible SIRVA). For repeated or depot injections, rotate and document the site to prevent fibrosis and induration.

tips: Lot number and VIS date aren't busywork — they're how a vaccine is traced if a recall or reaction occurs.
Recording the site each time makes rotation automatic for the next clinician giving a depot medication.
Tell the patient which symptoms mean 'call us' before they leave — most reactions happen at home.
mistakes: Omitting lot number, expiration, or VIS date for a vaccine.
Not recording the exact site and side.
Skipping the observation period.
Failing to document rotation for repeated injections.

quiz.q: Which element is legally required specifically for vaccine documentation?
opts: Manufacturer, lot number, expiration, and VIS date
quiz.why: Vaccine records must include manufacturer, lot, expiration, and the VIS edition/date under federal requirements.

quiz.q: After a routine vaccine, you should observe the recipient for about:
quiz.why: About 15 minutes catches immediate reactions such as syncope or early anaphylaxis (30 minutes with an anaphylaxis history).

quiz.q: For a patient on a monthly depot injection, documenting the site each visit primarily supports:
opts: Systematic site rotation to prevent fibrosis
quiz.why: A documented site history lets the next clinician rotate and avoid repeated trauma to one spot.

takeaways: Record med, dose, route, exact site/side, needle, technique, response.
Vaccines: manufacturer, lot, expiration, VIS date — legally required.
Observe ~15 minutes (30 with anaphylaxis history).
Document rotation for repeated/depot injections.
lessons.refs: CDC Vaccine Administration; National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act; StatPearls.

lessons.title: Special populations
objectives: Adapt IM technique for pediatric and geriatric patients
Adjust needle length and site for obesity
Manage anticoagulated and thrombocytopenic patients
Apply comfort measures across ages
lessons.lead: Body size and physiology change the site, needle, and precautions. The goal is always the same — reach muscle safely — but the route there differs.

p: Use the vastus lateralis for infants and the deltoid once muscle is adequate (toddler and up). Follow the CDC needle-length guidance (16 mm neonates only if the skin is stretched; 25 mm for infants). Lean hard on comfort measures: breastfeeding or sucrose for infants, upright/parent-held positioning, distraction, and giving the most painful injection last. Don't aspirate — it adds pain.

Reduced muscle mass (sarcopenia) can make the deltoid too small — prefer the ventrogluteal, and re-assess needle length (thinner muscle but sometimes more fat). Absorption is slower, and thin skin and fragile vessels raise hematoma risk, so apply gentle pressure and watch the site.

Subcutaneous fat often exceeds a standard needle length, causing inadvertent subcutaneous delivery. Use a 1½-inch (38 mm) needle and favor the ventrogluteal, which reaches muscle more reliably than the deltoid or dorsogluteal in higher-BMI patients.

IM is relatively contraindicated. If unavoidable, obtain the order, use the smallest gauge and the ventrogluteal, apply firm pressure for 5–10 minutes without massage, and monitor for hematoma. For vaccines in anticoagulated patients, use a fine needle (≤23 G), firm pressure ≥2 minutes, and no rubbing.

note.t: Across ages, a too-short needle in a higher-BMI patient is the commonest cause of a failed 'IM' injection landing in fat.

tips: In obesity, choose the ventrogluteal and a 1½-inch needle by default — it's the most reliable path to muscle.
For children, the comfort plan (position, distraction, no aspiration, painful shot last) matters as much as the technique.
In frail older adults, the deltoid may simply be too small — the ventrogluteal is the safer bet.
mistakes: Using a standard-length needle in an obese patient and depositing in fat.
Insisting on the deltoid in a sarcopenic older adult.
Massaging an injection site in an anticoagulated patient.
Aspirating in children, adding needless pain.

quiz.q: A patient with obesity (BMI 40) needs an IM antibiotic. Best site and needle?
quiz.why: The ventrogluteal with a 1½-inch needle reliably reaches muscle through thick fat; shorter needles and the deltoid often land subcutaneously.

quiz.q: In a frail older adult with significant deltoid muscle loss, the better site is often:
quiz.why: Sarcopenia can make the deltoid inadequate; the ventrogluteal offers reliable muscle mass.

quiz.q: For a child receiving multiple injections, a correct comfort measure is:
opts: Give the most painful injection last and use distraction
Inject as fast as possible without positioning
quiz.why: Distraction, positioning, and sequencing the most painful last reduce distress; aspiration only adds pain.

takeaways: Infants: vastus lateralis; correct needle length per CDC.
Obesity: ventrogluteal + 1½-inch needle to reach muscle.
Sarcopenic elders: prefer ventrogluteal over a small deltoid.
Anticoagulated: smallest gauge, prolonged pressure, no massage.
lessons.refs: CDC IM Injection guidance; StatPearls; geriatric IM technique literature.

lessons.title: Practice scenarios & case studies
objectives: Apply IM principles to realistic situations
Select site, needle, and technique together
Prioritize safety under time pressure
lessons.lead: Three cases integrate site selection, needle choice, aspiration, and complication recognition. Decide first, then check the reasoning.

sections.h: Case A — Obese adult, 3 mL antibiotic
p: A 45-year-old man, BMI 38, is ordered a 3 mL IM antibiotic.

note.t: Move: choose the ventrogluteal (holds 3 mL, thin fat, no major neurovasculature) with a 1½-inch, 21–22 G needle to clear the fat. Z-track, 90°, inject slowly (~10 sec/mL). The deltoid is wrong — far too small for 3 mL.

p: A 30-year-old woman, 68 kg, needs a 0.5 mL flu vaccine.

note.t: Move: deltoid, 1-inch (25 mm), 22–25 G needle, 2–3 fingerbreadths below the acromion, no aspiration, observe 15 minutes. The ventrogluteal is unnecessary for a small-volume vaccine.

p: A 70-year-old on warfarin (INR 3.5) has an IM medication ordered.

note.t: Move: IM is relatively contraindicated. Clarify the order and consider an alternate route. If IM is truly required, use the ventrogluteal, the smallest gauge, firm pressure for 5–10 minutes without massage, and monitor for hematoma.

quiz.q: Case A recap — why not the deltoid for the 3 mL antibiotic?
opts: The deltoid is only for children
The deltoid holds ~1–2 mL max — 3 mL needs the ventrogluteal
quiz.why: Volume drives the choice: the small deltoid can't hold 3 mL, while the ventrogluteal can.

quiz.q: Case B recap — should you aspirate before the flu vaccine?
opts: No — aspiration isn't recommended for vaccines
quiz.why: Vaccines are given without aspiration at recommended sites; it adds pain with no benefit.

quiz.q: Case C recap — the safest first step for the anticoagulated patient is:
opts: Give the IM quickly in the deltoid
Clarify the order / consider an alternate route
quiz.why: Anticoagulation is a relative contraindication; clarify and consider alternatives before committing to IM.

takeaways: Volume and BMI drive site and needle choice — 3 mL means ventrogluteal + 1½ in.
Vaccines: deltoid, correct landmark, no aspiration, 15-minute watch.
Anticoagulation: clarify first; minimize bleeding if IM is unavoidable.
Technique (Z-track, 90°, slow) is constant across cases.
lessons.refs: CDC Vaccine Administration; StatPearls; Perry, Potter & Ostendorf.

lessons.title: Subcutaneous tissue & absorption
objectives: Locate the subcutaneous layer relative to skin and muscle
Explain why SubQ gives slow, sustained absorption
List the factors that speed or slow absorption
State the SubQ volume limits
lessons.lead: Subcutaneous injection deposits medication into the fat layer beneath the skin, where a limited blood supply gives slow, steady absorption — exactly what insulin and heparin need.

p: The subcutaneous layer (hypodermis) is the loose connective and adipose tissue that lies below the dermis and above muscle and fascia. A subcutaneous injection targets this fat — not the muscle beneath it.

The subcutis is less vascular than muscle, so drugs are absorbed slowly and at a sustained rate. That predictable, prolonged curve is why insulin and heparin are given subcutaneously rather than as a rapid IM or IV bolus.

list: Site — for insulin, the abdomen absorbs fastest, then arms, thighs, and buttocks.
Blood flow / temperature — heat, massage, and exercising the injected limb speed absorption (a hypoglycemia risk if a patient injects the thigh, then exercises).
Lipohypertrophy — thickened, repeatedly-injected tissue blunts and scrambles absorption.
Perfusion — edema, shock, and vascular disease slow and make uptake unpredictable.

p: The low-vascularity, low-compliance tissue tolerates only small volumes: ≤1 mL in adults and ≤0.5 mL in children. Larger volumes are painful and poorly absorbed.

note.t: Because site blood flow drives uptake, teaching a patient to keep the same body region for a given insulin (e.g., abdomen for consistency) reduces day-to-day glucose swings.

tips: Remember the ranking for insulin: abdomen is fastest, buttock slowest — useful when timing matters.
If a patient's glucose is unpredictable, ask where and how they inject before adjusting the dose — technique is often the cause.
Small volume is the rule: over ~1 mL, reconsider the route or split the dose.
mistakes: Injecting more than ~1 mL into subcutaneous tissue.
Ignoring that exercise or heat over the site speeds absorption.
Assuming absorption is uniform across sites.
Injecting into poorly perfused, edematous tissue.

quiz.q: A patient injects rapid-acting insulin into the thigh, then goes for a run and becomes hypoglycemic. The mechanism is:
opts: The insulin dose was too high
Exercise increased blood flow to the injected limb, speeding absorption
Insulin doesn't work in the thigh
quiz.why: Increased local blood flow from exercise accelerates subcutaneous absorption, intensifying the insulin effect.

quiz.q: Subcutaneous medications absorb slowly and steadily because the tissue is:
quiz.why: The limited blood supply of the subcutis produces a slow, sustained absorption curve.

quiz.q: The usual maximum volume for an adult subcutaneous injection is about:
quiz.why: Subcutaneous tissue tolerates only small volumes — about 1 mL in adults.

takeaways: SubQ tissue is fat below the dermis, above muscle.
Low vascularity → slow, sustained absorption (ideal for insulin/heparin).
Site, heat, exercise, and lipohypertrophy all change uptake.
Keep volumes ≤1 mL (adult), ≤0.5 mL (child).
lessons.refs: Open RN Nursing Skills (Subcutaneous); FITTER recommendations; ADA Standards of Care.

lessons.title: Syringes, needles & pens
objectives: Match syringe type to the medication (insulin vs tuberculin)
Select needle gauge and length
Apply the short-needle (4 mm) evidence
Handle insulin pens and prefilled enoxaparin correctly
lessons.lead: The right syringe and a short needle make subcutaneous injections both accurate and comfortable. Two devices — insulin pens and prefilled enoxaparin syringes — have specific handling rules.

p: Insulin syringes are calibrated in units and must match the insulin concentration — a U-100 syringe with U-100 insulin, and the dedicated U-500 syringe with U-500 insulin. A mismatch is a dangerous dosing error. Tuberculin syringes (1 mL, fine graduations) are used for small non-insulin volumes.
Subcutaneous needles are typically 25–31 G and short — traditional lengths ½–⅝ inch, and modern pen needles 4–8 mm, with 4–6 mm now standard.

note.t: FITTER recommends the shortest needle (4 mm pen / 6 mm syringe) for ALL patients regardless of age, sex, or BMI — equal glycemic control, less pain, and less risk of accidental IM injection. With a 4 mm needle, a 90° angle without pinching is appropriate even in lean adults.

list: Insulin pens — single patient only; use a new needle each dose, prime ~2 units before dosing, and hold in place ~10 seconds after injecting.
Prefilled enoxaparin (Lovenox) — comes with a deliberate air bubble that must NOT be expelled; expelling it underdoses the patient.

tips: Default to a 4 mm pen needle — the evidence supports it for everyone, including patients with obesity.
Check the syringe units-marking matches the insulin concentration every single time; this is a classic error.
Leave the enoxaparin air bubble alone — it's there on purpose to deliver the full dose.
mistakes: Mismatching syringe to insulin concentration (e.g., U-100 syringe with U-500 insulin).
Expelling the air bubble from a prefilled enoxaparin syringe.
Sharing an insulin pen between patients.
Using an unnecessarily long needle and risking IM delivery.

quiz.q: Why does FITTER recommend a 4 mm pen needle at 90° even for patients with obesity?
opts: Longer needles are safer in obesity
4 mm reliably reaches fat but not muscle in all body sizes, with less pain and lower IM risk
Only thin patients need short needles
quiz.why: A 4 mm needle deposits reliably in subcutaneous fat regardless of BMI, avoiding the IM delivery that longer needles risk.

quiz.q: A U-100 insulin syringe is used to draw U-500 insulin. This is:
opts: A dangerous dosing error — the syringe must match the concentration
quiz.why: Concentration and syringe calibration must match; a mismatch causes a large dosing error.

quiz.q: A prefilled enoxaparin syringe has a small air bubble. You should:
opts: Tap it to the plunger and push it out
quiz.why: The air bubble is intentional; retaining it delivers the full dose and clears the needle.

takeaways: Insulin syringe units must match the insulin concentration.
Short needles (4 mm pen / 6 mm syringe) suit all patients.
4 mm needle → 90°, no pinch, even in lean adults.
Never expel the enoxaparin air bubble; pens are single-patient.
lessons.refs: FITTER (Mayo Clinic Proceedings); Open RN Nursing Skills; enoxaparin prescribing information.

lessons.title: Assessment & the rights
objectives: Confirm the rights, including double-checks for high-alert drugs
Screen contraindications by site and condition
Perform insulin-specific pre-checks
Assess readiness for patient self-injection
