lessons.lead: Insulin and heparin are high-alert medications — small errors cause serious harm. Assessment includes the rights, an independent double-check, and drug-specific safety checks.

p: Verify the right patient, drug, dose, route, time, plus documentation, reason, and response. Insulin and heparin are high-alert medications; many facilities require an independent second-nurse check of insulin doses. Confirm the concentration and units carefully.

list: Impaired perfusion — edema, cellulitis, poor circulation — gives unreliable absorption; avoid that area.
Bruised, scarred, hardened, or lipohypertrophic tissue — do not use.
Bleeding risk / anticoagulation — assess before heparin/LMWH: recent surgery, severe renal or hepatic disease, thrombocytopenia; review aPTT (heparin) and platelet count (enoxaparin, for HIT).

p: Check the blood glucose per order or sliding scale, and confirm the patient can and will eat before giving rapid- or short-acting insulin — otherwise you risk hypoglycemia. Screen for interacting drugs (aspirin, NSAIDs, thrombolytics increase bleeding with heparin).

note.t: Never give rapid-acting insulin to a patient who is not about to eat unless specifically ordered — timing insulin to food prevents hypoglycemia.

p: For home medications, assess vision, manual dexterity, and cognition; teach the technique and obtain a return demonstration before the patient injects independently.

tips: Treat insulin like the high-alert drug it is — the independent double-check catches unit and concentration errors before they reach the patient.
Confirm 'are you about to eat?' before rapid insulin; it's the simplest hypoglycemia prevention there is.
Teach-back and a return demonstration reveal gaps that a nodding 'yes, I understand' hides.
mistakes: Skipping the independent double-check on insulin.
Giving rapid insulin to a patient who won't eat.
Injecting into edematous or lipohypertrophic tissue.
Starting heparin without checking platelets/bleeding risk.

quiz.q: Before giving rapid-acting insulin, the most important check to prevent hypoglycemia is:
opts: Blood glucose and that the patient will eat
quiz.why: Rapid insulin timed to a meal prevents hypoglycemia; giving it to a patient who won't eat is dangerous.

quiz.q: Why do many facilities require an independent double-check for insulin?
opts: Insulin is high-alert and dosing/concentration errors cause serious harm
quiz.why: As a high-alert medication, insulin errors can be life-threatening, so a second verification is standard.

quiz.q: Before starting subcutaneous enoxaparin, a key lab to review is:
quiz.why: Platelets are checked for bleeding risk and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT).

takeaways: Insulin/heparin are high-alert — use independent double-checks.
Avoid edematous, scarred, or lipohypertrophic sites.
Check glucose and meal timing before rapid insulin.
Confirm self-injection readiness with a return demonstration.
lessons.refs: Open RN Nursing Skills; ISMP high-alert medications; enoxaparin prescribing information.

lessons.title: Site selection & rotation
objectives: Identify the acceptable subcutaneous sites
Rank insulin absorption by site
Apply site-rotation principles that prevent lipohypertrophy
Choose sites for heparin and enoxaparin
lessons.lead: Where you inject — and how you rotate — determines both absorption and long-term tissue health. Good rotation is the single best defense against lipohypertrophy.

list: Abdomen — below the costal margin to the iliac crest, staying more than 2 inches (5 cm) from the umbilicus
Anterior/lateral thigh
Back of the upper arm
Upper back/scapular and upper outer buttock

p: For insulin, absorption ranks abdomen (fastest) > arm > thigh > buttock (slowest). Keeping one region for a given insulin gives more consistent day-to-day results.

Insulin: pick one anatomical region for consistency, then rotate systematically within it, spacing injections about a finger-width (~1 cm) apart, to prevent lipohypertrophy. Heparin/enoxaparin: the abdomen is preferred, alternating left and right sides each dose to limit bruising.

note.t: Avoid injecting within 5 cm of the umbilicus, and skip bruised, scarred, inflamed, or lipohypertrophic tissue, and any area near a hernia, stoma, or incision.

inter.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 340 200"><rect x="120" y="24" width="100" height="150" rx="30" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><rect x="86" y="40" width="26" height="80" rx="13" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><rect x="228" y="40" width="26" height="80" rx="13" fill="#f3ecdb" stroke="#d9d5cc"/><ellipse class="hot" data-id="abdomen" cx="170" cy="120" rx="26" ry="18" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".85"/><circle class="hot" data-id="navel" cx="170" cy="120" r="5" fill="#0a2a24"/><ellipse class="hot" data-id="arm" cx="99" cy="70" rx="9" ry="14" fill="#C9A24B" opacity=".9"/><text x="150" y="122" fill="#0a2a24" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter">abdomen</text></svg>
inter.q: Tap a good place to start on the abdomen.

fb.abdomen: Correct — a fleshy abdominal site well away from the navel.
fb.navel: Too close to the navel — stay more than 2 inches (5 cm) away.
fb.arm: The back of the upper arm works too, but the question asked for the abdomen.

tips: Teach 'rotate within a region, ~1 cm apart' — it keeps absorption consistent and prevents lumps.
For enoxaparin, alternate abdominal sides each dose to spread out the bruising.
Inspect and palpate before injecting: a lump you skip today prevents an erratic dose tomorrow.
mistakes: Injecting repeatedly into the same favorite spot (causes lipohypertrophy).
Injecting within 5 cm of the umbilicus.
Using bruised, scarred, or lumpy tissue.
Rotating insulin across regions in a way that makes absorption unpredictable.

quiz.q: A type 1 diabetic always injects the same left periumbilical spot and now has a soft raised lump and unexplained low glucose readings. The problem and fix?
opts: Insulin is too strong — lower the dose only
Lipohypertrophy — stop using the lump, rotate systematically, and expect a possible dose review
quiz.why: Repeated same-spot injection causes lipohypertrophy and erratic absorption; rotating to healthy tissue may increase effect, so the dose may need review.

quiz.q: Which site absorbs insulin fastest?
quiz.why: Abdomen is fastest, followed by arm, thigh, then buttock.

quiz.q: On the abdomen, you should avoid injecting within about:
quiz.why: Stay more than 2 inches (5 cm) from the navel.

takeaways: Sites: abdomen, thigh, back of arm, upper back/buttock.
Insulin absorption: abdomen fastest, buttock slowest.
Rotate within a region ~1 cm apart to prevent lipohypertrophy.
Heparin/enoxaparin: abdomen, alternating sides; stay 5 cm from the navel.
lessons.refs: Open RN Nursing Skills; FITTER; ADA Standards of Care; enoxaparin prescribing information.

lessons.title: Preparation & asepsis
objectives: Apply hand hygiene and site antisepsis by setting
Prepare and resuspend insulin correctly
Mix insulins in the correct order
Avoid actions that alter the dose
lessons.lead: Subcutaneous preparation is straightforward, but two things trip people up: whether to use alcohol (it depends on the setting) and the correct order for mixing insulins.

p: Perform hand hygiene and wear gloves. Skin antisepsis is context-dependent: in the clinical/hospital setting, cleanse with an alcohol swab in a firm circular motion and let it dry. For routine home insulin into clean skin, alcohol prep is often omitted — and if used, it must dry first.

note.t: This isn't a contradiction — it's setting-driven. Clinical settings standardize alcohol antisepsis; routine home self-injection into clean skin may skip it. Either way, if alcohol is used, let it dry.

list: Cloudy insulin (NPH): gently roll the vial between your palms to resuspend — never shake.

Mixing NPH + regular ('clear before cloudy'): inject air into the NPH vial, then air into the regular vial, then draw up the clear/regular insulin first, then the cloudy NPH — so long-acting insulin never contaminates the fast-acting vial.
Never mix glargine (Lantus) or detemir (Levemir) with other insulins.

p: Do not massage the site before or after in a way that changes absorption, and never add or remove volume from a fixed prefilled device.

tips: 'Clear before cloudy' — draw the clear (regular) insulin first so you never seed the fast-acting vial with NPH.
Roll NPH, don't shake it — shaking creates bubbles and foam that throw off the dose.
In the home setting, don't insist on alcohol if the skin is clean; just ensure it dries if used.
mistakes: Shaking NPH instead of rolling it.
Drawing cloudy insulin before clear (contaminates the regular vial).
Mixing glargine or detemir with another insulin.
Injecting through wet alcohol.

quiz.q: You must give NPH and regular insulin together. The correct sequence is:
opts: Air into NPH, air into regular, draw regular (clear) first, then NPH
Mix them in a separate vial
quiz.why: 'Clear before cloudy' prevents long-acting NPH from contaminating the fast-acting regular insulin.

quiz.q: A hospital uses alcohol prep for a subcutaneous injection, but a home patient does not. This is:
opts: Setting-dependent and acceptable — clinical settings standardize alcohol; clean home skin may skip it
quiz.why: Both are accepted practice; the difference is setting-driven, and alcohol (if used) must dry first.

quiz.q: To resuspend cloudy NPH insulin you should:
opts: Gently roll it between your palms
quiz.why: Rolling resuspends without creating foam/bubbles that distort the dose.

takeaways: Alcohol prep is setting-dependent; if used, let it dry.
Roll NPH to resuspend — never shake.
'Clear before cloudy' when mixing regular + NPH.
Never mix glargine or detemir.
lessons.refs: Open RN Nursing Skills; ADA Standards of Care.

lessons.title: Pinch, angle & injection
objectives: Choose pinch and angle by needle length and body habitus
Apply the no-aspiration rule for insulin and heparin
Perform the injection sequence, including pens
Use the enoxaparin air-lock technique
lessons.lead: The core subcutaneous technique — a skin pinch and the right angle — adapts to the needle and the patient. A few drug-specific rules (no aspiration, no rubbing, the enoxaparin air-lock) prevent common errors.

p: Pinch a skinfold with the nondominant hand to lift subcutaneous tissue off the muscle, then choose your angle.
list: 90° for normal-size or obese adults, and whenever using a short (4–6 mm) needle.
45° for thin patients / minimal subcutaneous tissue, or when only a longer needle is available.
Exception: with a 4 mm pen needle, inject at 90° without pinching — the short needle can't reach muscle, and a pinch can push it into the dermis.

p: Aspiration is not necessary for subcutaneous injections, and you should never aspirate insulin or heparin/LMWH — it adds no safety benefit and, with anticoagulants, contributes to bruising. Pens and prefilled syringes have no aspiration step.

list: Insert quickly, then inject with steady, gentle pressure.
Release the skinfold and, for pens, wait ~10 seconds before withdrawing so the full dose delivers.
Withdraw at the same angle and apply gentle pressure with gauze — do NOT massage (massage after heparin causes hematoma).
Do not recap; activate the safety shield and dispose in a sharps container.

p: Position the patient supine. Do not expel the air bubble. No aspiration, no rubbing afterward. Inject into the abdominal wall, alternating sides, more than 5 cm from the umbilicus, holding the skinfold throughout and releasing it after injecting.

note.t: For heparin and insulin pens, keep the skinfold pinched throughout and release just before withdrawing. Never rub an enoxaparin or heparin site.

tips: Slow and gentle is what makes SubQ injections comfortable — there's no prize for speed.
With a 4 mm pen needle, resist the urge to pinch — 90° flat is correct and safer.
For any anticoagulant, hands off afterward: gentle pressure, absolutely no rubbing.
mistakes: Aspirating insulin or heparin.
Rubbing/massaging the site after heparin or enoxaparin.
Pinching a skinfold at an angle with a 4 mm pen needle (risks intradermal delivery).
Withdrawing a pen immediately instead of waiting ~10 seconds.

quiz.q: A patient on enoxaparin develops repeated abdominal bruising. Which two technique changes most directly reduce it?
opts: Stop rubbing the site and stop aspirating; alternate sides and keep the air bubble
quiz.why: Massage and aspiration traumatize capillaries; the no-rub, no-aspirate, air-lock, alternating-side technique minimizes bruising.

quiz.q: You are using a 4 mm insulin pen needle on an average adult. Correct technique is:
opts: Pinch a skinfold and inject at 45°
quiz.why: The 4 mm needle reaches fat but not muscle; 90° without a pinch is correct and avoids intradermal delivery.

quiz.q: For a lean elderly patient when only a ⅝-inch (8 mm) needle is available, the best adjustment is:
opts: Pinch a skinfold and inject at 45°
quiz.why: A pinch plus a 45° angle keeps the longer needle within subcutaneous fat and avoids IM delivery in thin tissue.

takeaways: Angle by needle/tissue: 90° short-needle/obese; 45° thin/long-needle; 4 mm = 90°, no pinch.
Never aspirate insulin or heparin.
Never rub after heparin/enoxaparin; use the air-lock, alternate sides, patient supine.
Slow, gentle injection = comfort.
lessons.refs: FITTER; Open RN Nursing Skills; enoxaparin prescribing information.

lessons.title: Complications & troubleshooting
objectives: Recognize and prevent lipohypertrophy and lipoatrophy
Manage bruising and bleeding with anticoagulants
Recognize and respond to hypoglycemia
Reduce injection pain and local reactions
lessons.lead: Most subcutaneous complications are preventable with rotation and correct technique. The two that demand quick recognition are hypoglycemia (insulin) and bleeding (anticoagulants).

p: Lipohypertrophy — rubbery, raised fatty tissue from repeated same-spot injection or needle reuse — blunts and scrambles absorption. Prevent it with systematic rotation and a new needle each time; manage it by stopping use of the area and re-educating (and expect an insulin dose review, since healthy tissue absorbs more). Lipoatrophy (dimpling/fat loss) is rarer with modern insulins.

head: Problem
Prevent / manage
rows: No rubbing, no aspiration, air-lock for enoxaparin, rotate sides; gentle pressure
Assess labs/bleeding risk first; hold pressure; report hematuria, melena, gum bleeding
Shortest needle, room-temp insulin, let alcohol dry, inject slowly

p: An insulin dosing error, an accidental IM injection, a missed meal, or exercising the injected limb can cause hypoglycemia. Recognize the signs — shakiness, sweating, confusion, tachycardia — and treat per protocol.

note.t: A dose that used to be 'right' can suddenly cause lows once a patient stops injecting into lipohypertrophy and moves to healthy tissue — anticipate a dose review.

tips: Rotation isn't cosmetic — it's what keeps insulin absorption (and glucose control) predictable.
With anticoagulants, expect some bruising, but rubbing or aspirating makes it much worse.
Cold insulin stings; letting it reach room temperature is a simple comfort win.
mistakes: Continuing to inject into lipohypertrophic tissue.
Rubbing an anticoagulant site and enlarging a hematoma.
Not recognizing early hypoglycemia after an insulin error.
Reusing needles, promoting lipohypertrophy and pain.

quiz.q: A patient's glucose becomes erratic and you palpate a rubbery raised area where they always inject. Best next step?
opts: Identify lipohypertrophy, move to healthy rotated tissue, and re-check whether the dose needs lowering
quiz.why: Healthy tissue absorbs more reliably, so moving off the lump may raise the effect — anticipate a possible dose reduction.

quiz.q: Which cluster suggests hypoglycemia after an insulin injection?
quiz.why: Adrenergic and neuroglycopenic signs — shakiness, sweating, confusion, tachycardia — indicate hypoglycemia.

quiz.q: The most effective way to prevent lipohypertrophy is:
opts: Systematic site rotation and a new needle each dose
quiz.why: Rotation and fresh needles prevent the repeated tissue trauma that causes lipohypertrophy.

takeaways: Rotation + fresh needles prevent lipohypertrophy and keep absorption predictable.
Anticoagulant sites: gentle pressure, never rub.
Know hypoglycemia signs and treat promptly.
Moving off lipohypertrophy may require an insulin dose review.
lessons.refs: ADA Standards of Care; FITTER; Open RN Nursing Skills.

lessons.title: Documentation & follow-up
objectives: Document all required elements including units and site
Record glucose and rotation for insulin
Document labs and site alternation for anticoagulants
Monitor for the right follow-up signs
lessons.lead: Subcutaneous documentation ties the dose to the site and the monitoring plan — especially important for high-alert insulin and anticoagulants.

list: Date/time, medication, dose (units for insulin), concentration
Site (and rotation), route, angle, and patient position
Who performed any independent double-check
Patient response, tolerance, and education provided

p: Insulin: document the pre-administration blood glucose, sliding-scale rationale, food intake, and rotation record. Anticoagulants: document relevant labs (aPTT for heparin; platelets for enoxaparin), the site side alternated, and any bruising.

After insulin, watch for hypoglycemia signs; after anticoagulants, watch for bleeding — hematuria, hematemesis, melena, and enlarging bruises. For self-injecting patients, document the teaching and the return demonstration.

tips: Always document insulin in units and note the concentration — 'units' plus the site closes the high-alert loop.
A documented rotation history is what lets the next nurse avoid the overused spot.
Record the glucose that justified a sliding-scale dose so the reasoning is transparent.
mistakes: Writing 'insulin given' without units, concentration, and site.
Omitting the pre-dose glucose for a sliding-scale insulin.
Not documenting which abdominal side got the enoxaparin.
Skipping documentation of self-injection teaching.

quiz.q: The most complete insulin documentation includes:
opts: Dose in units, concentration, site/rotation, pre-dose glucose, and response
quiz.why: Units, concentration, site, the justifying glucose, and response make a high-alert dose auditable and safe.

quiz.q: For subcutaneous enoxaparin, a useful thing to document each dose is:
quiz.why: Recording the side supports alternation and helps track bruising.

quiz.q: After giving insulin, appropriate follow-up monitoring focuses on:
quiz.why: Hypoglycemia is the key post-insulin risk to monitor and treat.

takeaways: Document insulin in units with concentration and site.
Record pre-dose glucose and rotation for insulin.
Record labs and alternated side for anticoagulants.
Monitor hypoglycemia (insulin) and bleeding (anticoagulants).
lessons.refs: Open RN Nursing Skills; ISMP; enoxaparin prescribing information.

lessons.title: Special populations
objectives: Adapt technique for pediatric and thin/geriatric patients
Approach patients with obesity
Support safe self-administration
Match needle and angle to body habitus
lessons.lead: The subcutaneous fundamentals hold across ages, but needle length, angle, and the pinch adjust to the patient's tissue — and self-injectors need real teaching.

p: Use the shortest needle (4 mm pen) and smaller volumes (≤0.5 mL). The thigh and abdomen are common. A skinfold pinch is often used in young or lean children; involve caregivers in teaching.
Thin subcutaneous tissue raises IM-injection risk. Pinch a skinfold, use a short needle, and use 45° if only a longer needle is available. Thin skin bruises easily — gentle pressure, no rubbing.

There is ample subcutaneous depth, so inject at 90°; 4 mm needles remain effective and preferred, supported by evidence in adults with obesity. Choose a site with accessible fat and avoid injecting into a skin crease.
Emphasize education, rotation logs, adherence, safe sharps disposal, single-patient pen use, and a return demonstration before independence.

note.t: The commonest thin-patient error is an accidental IM injection from too long a needle at 90° — pinch and angle to 45° when needed.

tips: Short needles solve most population differences — a 4 mm pen works from lean children to adults with obesity.
In thin or elderly patients, the pinch-and-45° combination is your safeguard against IM delivery.
Watch a self-injector actually do it once — the return demonstration catches what words miss.
mistakes: Using a long needle at 90° in a thin patient (risks IM injection).
Assuming patients with obesity need a longer needle — 4 mm still works.
Injecting into a skin crease.
Declaring a patient independent without a return demonstration.

quiz.q: A thin elderly patient needs a subcutaneous injection and only a ⅝-inch (8 mm) needle is stocked. Best technique?
opts: Pinch a skinfold and inject at 45°
quiz.why: A pinch plus 45° keeps the longer needle within subcutaneous fat and avoids IM delivery in thin tissue.

quiz.q: For a patient with obesity, the appropriate needle and angle are:
quiz.why: Ample subcutaneous depth means a 4 mm needle at 90° reliably reaches fat; longer needles aren't needed.

quiz.q: Before a patient self-injects at home independently, you should ensure:
quiz.why: A return demonstration confirms competence beyond verbal understanding.

takeaways: Short needles (4 mm) suit children through adults with obesity.
Thin/elderly: pinch and use 45° with a longer needle.
Obesity: 90° with a 4 mm needle; avoid skin creases.
Confirm self-injection competence with a return demonstration.
lessons.refs: FITTER; ADA Standards of Care; Open RN Nursing Skills.

lessons.title: Practice scenarios & case studies
objectives: Apply subcutaneous principles to realistic situations
Correct technique errors
Integrate site, angle, and drug-specific rules
lessons.lead: Three cases bring the course together across insulin and anticoagulants. Decide your move, then check the reasoning.

sections.h: Case A — Thin patient, long needle only
p: A thin elderly patient needs basal insulin, but only ⅝-inch (8 mm) syringe needles are available.

note.t: Move: pinch a firm skinfold and inject at 45° to keep the tip in fat and avoid an IM injection (which would speed absorption and risk hypoglycemia).

sections.h: Case B — Same-spot injector with lows
p: A type 1 diabetic always injects the same left periumbilical spot and reports a soft raised lump with unexplained hypoglycemia.

note.t: Move: identify lipohypertrophy. Stop injecting the lump, teach systematic rotation within the region (~1 cm apart) with a new needle each dose, and anticipate an insulin dose review — healthy tissue may absorb more and cause further lows.

p: A new nurse taps a prefilled enoxaparin syringe to expel the air bubble, plans to aspirate, and intends to rub the site afterward.

note.t: Move: correct all three errors. Do NOT expel the air bubble, do NOT aspirate, inject into the abdominal wall (alternating sides, >5 cm from the umbilicus) with the patient supine, hold the skinfold throughout, and do NOT rub afterward.

quiz.q: Case A recap — why pinch and use 45° with the ⅝-inch needle?
opts: To keep the longer needle in subcutaneous fat and avoid IM delivery
quiz.why: In thin tissue, a pinch plus a shallower angle prevents the needle reaching muscle.

quiz.q: Case B recap — moving off lipohypertrophy to healthy tissue may require:
opts: An insulin dose review, because absorption improves
quiz.why: Healthy tissue absorbs more reliably, so the previous dose may now cause lows.

quiz.q: Case C recap — which enoxaparin step is correct?
opts: Keep the air bubble, no aspiration, no rubbing, patient supine
quiz.why: The air-lock is retained, aspiration and rubbing are avoided, and the patient is supine.

takeaways: Thin tissue: pinch and 45° to avoid IM delivery.
Lipohypertrophy: rotate off it and expect a possible dose review.
Enoxaparin: keep the bubble, no aspirate, no rub, supine, alternate sides.
Drug-specific rules matter as much as general technique.
